blurb: The rules in this category are known in Pali as nissaggiya pācittiya, “entailing relinquishment and confession”. There are thirty such rules for the nuns, the same number as for the monks, although only eighteen are held in common. These rules, together with the remaining rules of the Monastic Code, are classed as light offenses, lahukāpatti. For light offenses there is usually no penalty apart from confession. For the nissaggiya pācittiyas, however, any item involved in the committing of an offense must be relinquished. Once the offense has been confessed, the item is, with a few exceptions, returned to the offender.

children.translated_name: 1. The training rule on collecting almsbowls
children.blurb: A nun who has more than one bowl must relinquish the extra bowl and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 2. Distributing out-of-season robe-cloth
children.blurb: A nun who distributes robe-cloth inappropriately must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 3. Taking back a traded robe
children.blurb: A nun who takes back a robe given in exchange to another nun must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 4. Asking for something else
children.blurb: A nun who asks for one thing but after receiving it says she wants something else must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 5. Exchanging for something else
children.blurb: A nun who gets one thing from a steward but then exchanges it for something else must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 6. Exchanging designated Sangha property
children.blurb: A nun who exchanges away a requisite belonging to the Order that is designated for a specific purpose must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 7. Exchanging designated Sangha property that was asked for
children.blurb: A nun who asks for a requisite belonging to the Order that is designated for a specific purpose and then exchanges it for something else must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 8. Exchanging designated collective property
children.blurb: A nun who exchanges away a collective requisite designated for a specific purpose must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 9. Exchanging designated collective property that was asked for
children.blurb: A nun who asks for a collective requisite designated for a specific purpose and then exchanges it for something else must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 10. Exchanging designated personal property that was asked for
children.blurb: A nun who asks for a personal requisite for a specific purpose and then exchanges it for something else must relinquish the item and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 11. Getting an expensive heavy cloak in exchange
children.blurb: A nun who asks for an expensive, heavy outer-robe must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 12. Getting an expensive light cloak in exchange
children.blurb: A nun who asks for an expensive, light outer-robe must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 13. The training rule on keeping extra robe-cloth beyond ten days
children.blurb: A nun who keeps extra robe-cloth for more than ten day must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 14. Staying apart from one’s robes
children.blurb: A nun who stays apart from any of her five robes for more than one day must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 15. Keeping extra robe-cloth beyond a month
children.blurb: A nun may keep extra robe-cloth for a month if she is expecting more. A nun who keeps it beyond that must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 16. Asking an unrelated person for a robe
children.blurb: A nun who asks a lay person for robe-cloth, except if her robes are lost or destroyed, must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 17. Accepting too many robes
children.blurb: When a nun’s robes are lost or destroyed, she should show moderation in asking for robe-cloth. If she does not, she must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 18. Requesting a fine robe from a fund
children.blurb: If a lay person wants to buy a robe for a nun, and the nun asks them for a fine robe, she must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 19. Requesting a fine robe from a combined fund
children.blurb: If two separate lay people want to buy a robe for a nun, and the nun asks them to put their money together to buy her a fine robe, she must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 20. Excessive prompting to get a robe
children.blurb: When money to be used for a nun’s robe has been placed with a steward in the proper way, that nun may hint to the steward that she needs a robe. If she does not get a robe in this way, but she does get one by acting inappropriately, she must relinquish the robe and confess the offense. This rule also prescribes the procedure through which a lay follower comes to hold money on behalf of a donor who wishes to support a nun.

children.translated_name: 21. Becoming the owner of money
children.blurb: A nun who receives money must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 22. Trading with money
children.blurb: A nun who receives money through trading must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 23. Bartering
children.blurb: A nun who barters, except with fellow monastics, must relinquish the item received and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 24. Exchanging an almsbowl with fewer than five mends
children.blurb: A nun who asks for a new bowl when her existing bowl is still useable must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 25. Storing the five tonics too long
children.blurb: A nun who keeps tonics for more than seven days must relinquish them and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 26. Taking back robe-cloth in anger
children.blurb: A nun who gives robe-cloth to another nun, but then takes it back in anger, must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 27. Asking for thread
children.blurb: A nun who asks for thread and then has robe-cloth woven with it must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 28. Bribing weavers
children.blurb: If a lay person is having robe-cloth woven for a nun and that nun makes demands of the weavers, then she must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 29. Storing haste-cloth too long
children.blurb: A nun who stores “an urgent robe” beyond the acceptable time limit must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 30. Diverting an offering intended for the Sangha
children.blurb: A nun who diverts to herself an offering intended for the Order must relinquish the item and confess the offense.
